judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are strong and nearly equivalent, but Report 2 edges ahead by explicitly adding a reproducibility check (re-running in assert mode at least twice) alongside naming the set-ordering instability source.

report_1.evidence: Per the file itself, they make real HTTP calls to `https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score` and "incur per-call cost"... Enabling them on the default run would make every test run cost money... The `not live` default should stay.

report_2.evidence: each makes a real HTTP POST to `https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score` authenticated with `SCORELAB_API_KEY`, and the module docstring states they "hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost." The deselection is intentional cost-gating, not a gap to fix.

report_1.evidence: regenerating the golden file now would silently bless the rounding change ... hand-verify the new expected scores ... then run `scripts/update_golden.py` and **review the resulting golden diff line by line** before committing

report_2.evidence: first I'd verify every changed value traces to an intended change, and here one does not cleanly ... Only after resolving that would I run `scripts/update_golden.py`, then diff the new golden against the old and hand-verify each score against the new weights

report_1.evidence: I would not cite the numbers from `docs/testing.md` — both are wrong against the current repo ... In the PR I would report the freshly measured numbers ... and note that `docs/testing.md` is stale and should be corrected.

report_2.evidence: I would not cite the numbers in `docs/testing.md` — both are stale; I'd re-measure and report my own measurements ... sourcing each figure from those commands rather than the doc.

re-run the suite in assert mode at least twice to confirm determinism ... `update_golden.py` iterates `CATEGORIES`, a Python `set` (the script's own comment says "set ordering is arbitrary") ... regenerating twice can produce spurious diffs
